preFix.title: Chain-plying on a drop spindle
preFix.subtitle: Plying a single strand into a 3-ply yarn using the chain-ply method — no second bobbin needed
preFix.excerpt: Chain-plying turns one cop of singles into a 3-ply yarn by looping the yarn through itself in successive chains, then plying the 3-strand loop back in the opposite direction. Colour sections from hand-painted tops stay intact as 3-ply sequences.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from general spinning references on Navajo-style plying, including Abby Franquemont 'Respect the Spindle' and online fibre community documentation of spindle-based chain-ply technique.

content.text: Chain-plying is a technique for creating a 3-ply yarn from a single
cop
of
singles

: no second spindle or bobbin is needed. The yarn is looped through itself in successive chains (like a crochet chain stitch made with yarn instead of hook), and the resulting 3-strand loop is plied back in the
S-twist

direction. The main practical advantage over 2-ply methods is that colour sections from hand-painted tops remain intact as 3-ply sequences rather than blending across two bobbins. The technique is also called Navajo-plying when done on a wheel. On a drop spindle it requires an extra hand or a brief park-and-pull for the chain formation, but the principle is identical.

items.name: Full cop of Z-spun singles on a drop spindle
Top-whorl or bottom-whorl drop spindle (same or second spindle)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Form the first chain loop

Park the spindle between the knees or under the arm so both hands are free. Pull about 60 cm of single from the cop. Fold the strand into a loop and reach through that loop with two fingers of the working hand to pull a new length of yarn through from the supply, exactly as in a crochet chain stitch but made with the yarn itself rather than a hook. Pull the new loop out to about 30 cm. This gives you a 3-strand section: two sides of the original loop plus the new yarn coming from the cop.
Step 2: Ply the 3-strand section
Hold the 3 strands together under consistent tension and spin the spindle counter-clockwise (for S-twist, which is the correct
chain-plying
direction for Z-spun singles). Allow the
S-twist

to travel up the 3-strand section. Keep the hands steady: if the 3 strands go slack, the twist will distribute unevenly and create a lumpy ply. Work down toward the spindle, letting the plied section wind onto the cop as it forms.
Step 3: Form the next chain

When the current 3-strand section is plied down to about 5 cm from the previous chain junction, park the spindle again. Reach through the existing loop with two fingers and pull the next length of yarn from the cop to form a new 30 cm loop. The junction between chains produces a slightly thicker spot; spacing chains at 25 to 30 cm intervals keeps these minimal. Continue plying the new 3-strand section as before.
Step 4: Check for balance
Every few chains, let a short length of the plied yarn hang free. A
balanced ply

hangs straight. If it coils back on itself, you are adding too much S-twist in the ply: give the spindle fewer turns per chain section. If the strands look loose and barely twisted together, add more turns before forming the next chain.
Step 5: Close the last loop and finish

When the cop is nearly exhausted, pull the remaining yarn through the final loop to close it rather than forming another chain. Ply the last short section as a 2-ply (two strands only) to the very end. Wind the finished plied yarn onto the niddy-noddy, tie in four places, and soak at 30 °C to set the twist.

items.fix: Pull longer loops: 30 to 40 cm per chain. The junction bump becomes proportionally smaller relative to the plied length.
items.cause: Chain loops too short, creating frequent junctions
items.problem: Thick bumps at every chain junction

items.fix: Hold all three strands between the fingers of the non-spinning hand, keeping equal tension on each before spinning the spindle.
items.cause: Uneven tension across the 3 strands during plying
items.problem: One strand pulls tight while the other two go slack

items.fix: Pull the loop out to at least 25 cm before beginning to ply. Keep the drafting hand well above the spindle so there is room to work.
items.cause: Loop too small or hands too close together
items.problem: Chain loop closes before fully plied

items.fix: Reduce the number of spindle turns per chain section. Test after each chain by letting the yarn hang free.
items.cause: Too many counter-clockwise turns per length of ply
items.problem: Finished yarn is over-twisted (coils on itself)
